schools.summary: Keil reads the raid as God refining David while arming him; Ellicott and Clarke fix Keilah's location and the raiders' habit of plundering threshing-floors.
Henry: David renders good for evil, a type of Christ; let God's church be quiet and the country will be quiet.
Wesley: the departure of God and David from Saul emboldened the Philistines; persecuting princes meet vexation on all sides.
Gill: the men of Keilah or their well-wishers reported the distress to David.
Keil says the inquiry was by Urim and Thummim; Ellicott argues it was through Gad the prophet, since Abiathar joined only at Keilah (v.6).
Clarke praises David's disinterested heroism in succouring Keilah rather than exploiting the invasion, and would set v.6 after v.1.
Gill: lacking Saul's commission and with a small force, David wisely inquired, probably through Gad.

debates.question: How did David inquire of the Lord — by the ephod or through the prophet?

sides.position: through Gad the prophet, the ephod not yet having arrived
through the Urim and Thummim of the high priest's ephod

schools.summary: Keil renders the men's double fear — Saul behind, Philistines before; Ellicott explains that the open country round Keilah, not the town, was under Philistine control.
Gill: the men were afraid even in the heart of Judah, dreading that Saul would fall on them.
Keil records the second answer added the express promise of victory; Ellicott: the renewed inquiry, through Gad, was to inspire the little army with confidence.
Clarke and Wesley: the second inquiry was for the soldiers' satisfaction, not David's.
Gill: David firmly believed already; he inquired again to remove his men's doubts and fears.
Gill: David went out animated by a commission from God and a promise of success, and so prevailed.
Clarke: 'brought away their cattle' means David recovered by reprisal the plunder the Philistines had driven off from round Keilah.
Keil sums the action in order: David fought, drove off the cattle, inflicted a severe defeat, and delivered Keilah.
Ellicott: the Hebrew is clear that Abiathar joined David only at Keilah; the earlier inquiries were through Gad, not the ephod, and the LXX altered the text to escape the difficulty.
Clarke would set v.6 after v.1 so the ephod is present throughout; Wesley recounts how Abiathar escaped Nob and carried the ephod away, of which Saul was justly deprived.
Gill: Abiathar fled to David at or near Keilah with the ephod.

debates.question: When did the ephod reach David, and by what means were the earlier inquiries made?

sides.position: Abiathar arrived only at Keilah; vv.2,4 were answered through Gad the prophet
the ephod was present from the first (v.6 belongs after v.1)

schools.summary: Henry: Saul rewards good with evil and abuses providence, calling his prey God's gift; one smiling providence never justifies an unrighteous cause.
Ellicott: a walled Keilah offered Saul his chance; the Hebrew says God has 'rejected/sold' David into his hand, yet Saul deludes himself his cause is God's.
Gill: the report of David's good deed, which should have reconciled Saul, only fed his malice.

themes: the trap of the walled city

schools.summary: Keil: Saul mustered the men of war to besiege David; Clarke: to scour the country; Ellicott: the alleged plea was a national war on the Philistines, masking the real design.
Gill: Saul summoned the people by herald, the royal prerogative of proclaiming war.

themes: the nation made an instrument of malice

schools.summary: Keil: David heard Saul was 'forging' mischief and inquired one question at a time; Ellicott corrects 'secretly,' suspects Jonathan's intelligence, and describes the twelve-gemmed ephod used as an oracle.
Gill: Saul gave out war on the Philistines while designing to take David at Keilah.
Clarke supposes David himself, clothed with the ephod, consulted the Lord.
Gill: David spoke, but the priest put the questions in the inquirer's name before God.
Keil sets the solemn address in place as the first half of a two-part inquiry.
Henry: the prayer is solemn, particular, and unselfish — David fears the city's ruin for his sake more than his own danger.
Clarke and Wesley: the oracle's answers were conditional ('if thou continue here'), proving contingency and free agency; Wesley adds the answer came by a voice, not by shining stones.
Ellicott studies the inverted questions and the Talmud's rule that only the first question asked is answered, so David repeated it in order.
Gill: the questions were put out of order, betraying David's perturbation of mind.

debates.question: How did the Urim and Thummim deliver its answer?

sides.position: by a voice or inward suggestion to the high priest
by some peculiar shining of the engraved stones (the common belief)

themes: God's knowledge of what would be

schools.summary: Gill: those who would betray David were the lords, governor, and chief magistrates of Keilah, in whose power the gates lay.
Henry: God knows men better than they know themselves — not only what will be but what would be if not prevented — and so delivers the godly out of temptation.
Ellicott: David's force had swelled to six hundred and moved without fixed plan in perpetual border warfare; Keil renders 'they wandered where they could go safely.'
Gill: the six hundred mark a gain of two hundred since Adullam, with no loss from the Philistine battle.
Henry: David followed Providence not knowing whither; God delivered him out of Saul's hand as a bird from the fowler's snare, and Saul's plot collapsed.

themes: the growing band of six hundred

schools.summary: Henry: David keeps God's way and waits God's time rather than strike Saul; the flesh persecutes the spirit, but Saul cannot prevail unless God grant it.
Keil and Ellicott locate the wilderness of Ziph and stress that though Saul sought daily, the invisible King of Israel would not give David into his hand.
Gill: David lodged in the high, strong rocks of the wilderness of Ziph.
Keil and Ellicott discuss whether the word means 'a wood/thicket' or the proper name 'Horesh,' noting no trace of the wood survives, and link the verse to Jonathan's coming.
Gill: David saw Saul's pursuit either from the mountain-top or, more likely, by information from friends such as Jonathan or his own spies.
Keil sets Jonathan's fidelity against the Ziphites' treachery and notes the renewed covenant; Ellicott shows how sorely David was tried and how Jonathan's sympathy and hope were a real help.
Wesley: Jonathan minded David of God's infallible promises and providence; Clarke vindicates Jonathan's loyalty, knowing God had appointed David yet not hastening the time.
Ellicott dwells on the pathos of 'I shall be next unto thee' — Jonathan would die on Gilboa; Saul now likely knew of David's anointing; this was their last meeting in life.
Wesley: Jonathan renounces his birthright, content to be second, bowing to God's appointment of David.
Gill: 'Fear not' bids David distrust neither God's power and promises nor dread Saul's wrath.
Gill: the covenant was a renewal, made in the name and fear of God and with him as witness.
Keil: a renewing by another solemn oath of the friendship already made, before Jonathan returned home.
Henry: true love delights to repeat its engagements — so our covenant with God should be often renewed.
Wesley: they parted here and never met again in this world.
Henry: the Ziphites betrayed their own tribesman and protector to flatter Saul, unbidden and without even asking a reward, worse than Judas in that.
Ellicott: the Ziphites were devoted adherents of Saul who kept their promise faithfully; 'Jeshimon' means the desert, the wilderness of Judea. Keil: for this Saul blessed them.
Gill: the Ziphites, terrified by the fate of Nob, informed on David, who then wrote a psalm of complaint (Gill, the 59th).
Wesley: David calls the Ziphites 'strangers' in the 54th Psalm, though of his own tribe, for their barbarous usage.

outliers.note: Assigns the psalm occasioned by this betrayal to the 59th, where Henry and Wesley refer it to the 54th.

schools.summary: Keil renders the fawning Hebrew — the doubled 'come down' and 'it is in us to deliver him' — exposing the informers' eagerness to gratify the king.
Gill: the Ziphites invite Saul down from Gibeah to their wilderness where David lay.
Henry: they not only reported David's place but undertook his capture, unbidden and unrewarded.

themes: flattery of a king's evil desire
the same knowledge, love or betrayal

schools.summary: Henry: 'so near is God to his mouth, though far from his heart' — Saul's self-pity and pious words are hypocrisy that only deceive.
Keil: Saul's evil conscience cast David as his would-be murderer, so he thanked God in delusion for the Ziphites' 'compassion.'
Gill: Saul commends and blesses the Ziphites and asks God's blessing on them for their offer.

themes: the language of piety without the heart

schools.summary: Keil: anxious lest David escape, Saul orders a careful reconnaissance so he not be deceived by false reports.
Gill: Saul sends the Ziphites home to prepare and to inquire more diligently and exactly after David.
Henry: Saul brands David as 'crafty to do mischief,' whereas all his subtlety was only to secure himself.
Keil gives Saul's resolve to search out David among all the families of Judah; Ellicott glosses 'thousands' as the greater tribal divisions, Judah being David's own tribe.
Gill: Saul demands an exact map of David's most-frequented lurking-places.
Henry: Saul now thought himself sure of his prey, pleased with the thought of devouring it.
Keil and Ellicott locate Maon (Main) south of Hebron near Ziph; Ellicott adds that the exact topography was preserved out of Israel's intense interest in David's wanderings.
Gill: the Ziphites went ahead of Saul to prepare, not in his company.
Keil identifies 'the rock' as the conical mountain of Maon, down which David went as Saul approached.
Gill: Saul with his army came out in search of David.
Clarke places Maon in the far south of Judah; Wesley takes 'a rock' as a cave from which David removed at Saul's approach.

themes: the rock buys time not safety

schools.summary: Keil: David had almost inextricably fallen into Saul's hands as the ring closed; Clarke: Saul pressed on, not knowing David was on the other side of the mountain. Gill: only one mountain lay between them.
Henry: the mountain was an emblem of Providence coming between David and the destroyer, like the pillar of cloud between Israel and Egypt.

themes: the mountain as emblem of Providence

schools.summary: Clarke: God had prepared the Philistine invasion and kept Saul ignorant, so the messenger came just in time — a visible divine interposition; Wesley: God's wisdom is never at a loss to preserve his people.
Ellicott (with Lange): this was God's plan to save David; the Philistines used Saul's southward withdrawal to invade the northern provinces.
Gill: whether the messenger was a courtier or (as the Jews say) an angel, God's providence timed his arrival exactly.
Ellicott and Keil derive Sela-hammahlekoth from a root meaning 'to be smooth' — the cliff of slipping away or escaping — as better supported than 'divisions.'
Clarke reads 'the rock of divisions' — the Targum's divided heart of the king, and Saul's forced separation from David to meet the Philistines.
Gill: Saul stopped short at once on the message and turned from David.

debates.question: What does the name Sela-hammahlekoth mean?

sides.position: 'the rock of divisions' — Saul's divided heart and his forced separation from David
'the rock of smoothness/escaping' — where David slipped free

outliers.note: Fancifully renders the name 'the Cliff of Destiny or of Fate,' a reading the commentators set aside.

schools.summary: Keil records the move to the strongholds of En-gedi; Clarke describes the cavern-riddled coast near the Dead Sea, the 'kid's well,' famed for vineyards and balm.
Gill: David went up from Maon after a narrow escape for his life.
Henry (with Lightfoot): here David penned Psalm 63, keeping up communion with God in all conditions.

themes: communion with God in the wilderness
